  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I haven't used social media in years, so I don't have much insight into this topic. But from what little I know through my children who are always talking about it, the best way is probably to ignore it completely unless you're sure something's wrong. People often forget that there are other ways to stay connected besides these platforms—like phone calls or emails if you want to be traditional. My dad would say that just like in real life where you might encounter someone pretending to be a friend, on social media too, sometimes the best thing is not even bother with it.\n\nThat said, I remember reading somewhere about how some people filter their feeds to avoid misinformation. Maybe there's an app or tool for that? Though my limited exposure suggests most folks just don't think much of it and let things slide unless they're personally affected. It also reminds me of how in church we talk about discernment—knowing who is telling the truth—and I suppose social media should be no different.\n\nBut honestly, since I'm retired and not online at all, my advice might sound a bit out of touch. Still, if you're going to deal with fake content, maybe set clear boundaries: only follow people or pages that have a reputation for being reliable. And when something does catch your eye as suspicious, don't jump on it right away—take time like I do before making important decisions.\n\nI think my children would disagree and say I'm too old-school in this, but they're young adults so maybe they're just more tech-savvy than me.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I haven't used social media in years, so I don't have much insight into this topic. But from what little I know through my children who are always talking about it, the best way is probably to ignore it completely unless you're sure something's wrong. People often forget that there are other ways to stay connected besides these platforms—like phone calls or emails if you want to be traditional. My dad would say that just like in real life where you might encounter someone pretending to be a friend, on social media too, sometimes the best thing is not even bother with it.

That said, I remember reading somewhere about how some people filter their feeds to avoid misinformation. Maybe there's an app or tool for that? Though my limited exposure suggests most folks just don't think much of it and let things slide unless they're personally affected. It also reminds me of how in church we talk about discernment—knowing who is telling the truth—and I suppose social media should be no different.

But honestly, since I'm retired and not online at all, my advice might sound a bit out of touch. Still, if you're going to deal with fake content, maybe set clear boundaries: only follow people or pages that have a reputation for being reliable. And when something does catch your eye as suspicious, don't jump on it right away—take time like I do before making important decisions.

I think my children would disagree and say I'm too old-school in this, but they're young adults so maybe they're just more tech-savvy than me.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:

2. Derive opinion from limited first-hand knowledge and rely on family insights.
